[TITLE]: I came across this image on social media and it upset me deeply. It's not about the politics. It's the way society depicts disability. This is a reprehensible statement. People with… | Onyinye Udokporo MA | 13 comments
[METADATA/INTRO]: I came across this image on social media and it upset me deeply. It's not about the politics. It's the way society depicts disability. This is a reprehensible statement. People with disabilities should never be used to make unkind comments....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: I came across this image on social media and it upset me deeply. It's not about the politics. It's the way society depicts disability. This is a reprehensible statement. People with disabilities should never be used to make unkind comments. Let's do better. Let's be kinder. Let's have zero tolerance for ableism. 
 Thank you for sharing this Onyinye It’s deeply upsetting to see disability used in this way, especially when so many disabled people already move through systems and spaces where they are misunderstood, dismissed, or dehumanised. You are right to name that this goes beyond politics. Using disability as an insult reinforces harmful attitudes towards people with visible and non-visible disabilities, and that has real impact. Posts like this remind us how much work there still is to do, and how society views disabled people.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: July 18th, 2019
[TITLE]: Cut Homeless People In Half by 2025 | Know Your Meme
[METADATA/INTRO]: | Navigation | | About • Origin • Spread • Various Examples • Search Interest • External References • Recent Images |...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: | About • Origin • Spread • Various Examples • Search Interest • External References • Recent Images | 
 Cut Homeless People In Half by 2025 refers to a satirical advertisement created by British street artist Fokawolf which looks like an advertisement from the UK Conservative Party stating "We plan to cut all homeless people in half by 2025." Once the advertisement spread on social media, many who did not know it was a parody began joking as if the UK Conservative Party had intentionally created the ad and misstated their actual plan to cut homelessness in half. This led to image macros and parodies of the advertisement.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2024-07-25
[TITLE]: ‘If people are upset, we’re doing something right’: the artists subverting the language of ads | Art | The Guardian
[METADATA/INTRO]: Labour’s landslide win in the general election saw many Conservative MPs experience a Portillo moment. Top Tories including Liz Truss, Penny Mordaunt and Jacob Rees-Mogg were unseated by discontented constituents. But, a few days before, something a wee bit different...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: Wolf agrees: “As soon as you put art into the format of advertising it’s a language everyone can relate to, which is fucking dark. It’s comforting for people. Objects have power.” 
 So much so, the collectives believe that it is possible to change people’s perceptions through their pranks. “It’s nice to put a spin or clever twist on something familiar and make someone do a double take and maybe question their perspective,” says Imbue. 
 With Labour winning in a landslide, the polling booth portable toilet turned out to be a portent of the Conservatives’ own journey down the pan. But what do the subvertisers think of the election result?
